data.title: Axos Financial: A Very Well Managed Bank
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Axos Financial Inc (NYSE:AX) is a regional U.S. bank operating nationwide, headquartered in Las Vegas, Nevada. AX offers a broad range of loan products such as residential mortgages, commercial loans, auto loans and consumer loans. The bank also provides wealth management services. The bank has delivered strong net interest margins while achieving a modest nonperforming loans to total loans ratio with an appropriate amount of leverage for the bank’s risk profile. When we consider overhead expenses, AX manages overhead expenses well compared to its competitors with an above-average efficiency ratio. Furthermore, the bank has outperformed its competitors in terms of return on assets and return on equity, illustrating AX’s proficiency to propel shareholders' equity, making the bank more valuable over time. The valuation implies an estimated five-year annual return of 18.0%, making AX a buy.

Net Interest Margin

Axos Financial has reached an updated median NIM throughout the previous decade of 4.2%.

While contrasting AX’s ten-year median NIM in comparison to its key competitors using the most current quarterly data reveals that Customers Bancorp (CUBI), First Interstate BancSystem (FIBK) and Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) each possess a ten-year median NIM of 3.0%, 3.4%, and 2.7%, respectively. This ranks AX as possessing the highest decade-long median NIM.

If we turn our focus to the near-term NIM trend, it can be seen that Axos Financial achieved a net interest margin of 4.9% in the quarter just concluded versus 4.3% in the corresponding quarter last year. This equates to a YoY growth rate of 15.8%, as opposed to CUBI, FIBK, and ASB, which reported YoY NIM growth of -5.8%, -2.5% and -2.6%, respectively. This ranks AX as realizing the highest NIM growth, therefore outperforming competing banks.

In the case of AX, the ten-year standard deviation of NIM is 0.00338, which ranks it as having the third lowest NIM standard deviation amongst its peers. Therefore, in my opinion, this shows that AX recently it seems that the bank’s net interest margins have not been as stable compared to its competitors, therefore expect greater volatility within the NIMs.

While assessing the NIM standard deviation over the short term, Axos Financial recorded a trailing 12-month standard deviation while incorporating the most recent quarterly data of 0.0026300, ranking AX as the most volatile compared to similar banks. This reflects that the bank’s loan book has been less steady compared to its competitors, however one should note that this volatility has been to the upside.

Nonperforming Loans to Total Loans Ratio

As evident from the information below with the recent quarterly data included, over the previous ten years AX has witnessed their NPL ratio span between 1.20% and 0.40%. When we put this alongside the banking industry, Axos Financial from 2016 to the last twelve months outperformed the banking sector as a whole. This signals that Axos Financial, through the preceding ten years, has demonstrated nonperforming loans to total loans ratio that outperform the broader banking industry while at the same time outperforming in terms of net interest margins. This shows that the management team has done a stellar job at minimizing balance sheet risk while also delivering a strong upside on their loans. In the last year, we can see that AX has outperformed the banking industry. This is because the bank has shown strong lending discipline despite being able to drive a solid net interest margin in the last twelve months.

While contrasting AX’s ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio in relation to its competitors, it is clear that CUBI, FIBK, and ASB every one of them has a ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio (inclusive of the latest quarterly numbers) of 0.25%, 0.65%, and 0.55%; thus AX is positioned second within this assessment. In my view, since AX has a ten-year median NPL ratio of 0.55%, I think that AX is well positioned to achieve acceptable net interest margins given the low level of risk that their loan book presents.

Leverage

Axos Financial has achieved and maintained a ten-year median asset to equity ratio (with the most recent quarterly metrics factored in) of 10.3. From 2016 to the trailing twelve months, the data shows that Axos Financial’s assets to equity ratio has shown some variability, where in 2016 the assets to equity ratio was 11.12 and in the last twelve months the assets to equity ratio was 9.40. Through this period, the data shows that AX’s assets to equity ratio reached a peak of 11.25 in 2020, while also finding a low of 9.40 in TTM. Since 2022, we have seen the management team dial back loan book leverage because of the recent interest rate rises. In my perspective, the overall trend suggests that the bank has become less aggressive in the past decade in terms of leverage, which is a conservative approach that helps to de-risk the bank’s balance sheet, particularly given the current macro environment. In this quarter, the assets to equity ratio is currently 8.9% below the ten-year median assets to equity ratio.

In our observations, Axos Financial has the third-lowest degree of ten-year median leverage in comparison to its peers, and taking into account that AX also has a better than average nonperforming loans to total loans ratio, this indicates that the management team are capable of managing balance sheet risk while simultaneously maintaining driving higher than average returns on equity through higher levels of leverage as demonstrated by their assets to equity ratio.

In the latest quarter, Axos Financial achieved year-over-year growth of -9.6%, suggesting that in the near term, AX’s leverage has been in a downward indicating that the immediate strategy is to decrease the bank’s leverage, decreasing risk slightly but also positioning the bank to lower their ROE.

Efficiency Ratio

I think the efficiency ratio is a significant metric in indicating how effectively banks manage their operational expenses, and in the banking field, an efficiency ratio less than 60% is generally considered preferable. AX’s efficiency ratio from 2016 through to the present quarter has been below industry benchmarks, where the efficiency ratio in 2016 began at 34% and then worsened to 46%. We can also observe that the efficiency ratio bottomed out at 34% in 2016 and got as high as 51% in 2019. Since 2016, AX has achieved success in documenting a total of ten years throughout the last decade in which the efficiency ratio stayed below 60%, resulting in a median efficiency ratio over ten years of 47%. This shows that the bank's management team has done an excellent job in minimizing overhead expenses that would eat into net profit margins.

In the process of comparing AX’s ten-year median efficiency ratio in comparison to its counterparts, it is clear that CUBI, FIBK, and ASB all maintain a ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio of 57%, 61%, and 66%, where AX is ranked the lowest alongside its competing entities. As I see it, when set against the backdrop of other similar banks, the management team has demonstrated that they have done an effective job at managing overhead costs at the bank.

Deposits and Funding Sources

Axos Financial, over the last decade, has successfully kept a loan to deposit ratio of under 100% in only two of the past ten years. This shows that the bank is reliant on other more expensive funding sources, such as Federal Home Loan Bank Advances, to meet the demand of the loan book. I do believe that since the bank often relies on alternative funding sources, the management team should look to improve the deposits side of the business.

Focusing on the latest quarter, Axos Financial achieved a loan to deposit ratio of 101% compared to 104% in the same period of the prior year. This shows a 3.0% decrease in the loan to deposit ratio. Since the loan to deposit ratio this quarter is greater than 100%, this implies that Axos Financial's access to cheap fund sources will be problematic as the ratio is above 100%.

Return on Assets and Return on Equity

Axos Financial, over the past ten years, when including the latest quarterly numbers, has had a median return on assets of 1.7%, whilst achieving a ten-year median return on equity of 17.1%. We can see that in 2016, both the ROA and ROE were 1.8% and 19.6%, respectively. We can also observe that ROA peaked at 2.1% in 2024 before returning to an ROA of 1.9% in the last twelve months, while the ROE reached a height of 21.4% in 2024 before seeing an ROE of 18.8% in the last twelve months.

When making a comparison of AX compared to its fellow banks across the recent ten-year span, it is clear that AX has the first highest rate of return on assets and the first greatest return on equity. It is important to consider that a higher return on equity is not always superior to a lower return on equity (although it usually is) as an astute analyst should consider how much extra risk (higher leverage or riskier loans) the bank took, if any to achieve the outsized returns on equity. Taking this into account, recognizing that return on assets and return on equity have trended higher during recent years versus previous years and since the executive team has effectively managed nonperforming loans to total loans ratio and loan book leverage, I consider the return on equity to be acceptable.

In the recent quarter, Axos Financial reported a ROA of 1.83% and a ROE of 17.56%, versus a ROA of 1.89% and a ROE of 19.81% in the year ago quarter, representing growth of -3.2% and -11.4%, respectively. This ranks Axos Financial as having the best ROA YoY growth trend and best ROE YoY growth trend amongst their peers. Given the negative recent growth trend and the competitive ranking amongst other comparable banks, I believe the management has shown effective ROE management despite negative YoY growth, though investors should remain cautious about this trend.

Valuation

In the process of determining the valuation for Axos Financial, I’ve made the decision to set the following conditions:

  • A normalised return on assets of 1.7% which is the ten-year median that encompasses a variety of market conditions.
  • A normalized assets to equity ratio of 10.3 constituting the ten-year median assets to equity ratio.
  • A normalized ten-year median payout ratio of 0%.
  • A current price to book ratio of 1.50 and a projected five-year future price to book ratio of 1.57, which is calculated using the ten-year median.

As evident from the above, I anticipate that Axos Financial throughout the following five years will offer shareholders a CAGR of 18.0% of which 17.0% is expected from book value growth, 0.9% is predicted from multiple expansion and an anticipated yearly dividend yield of 0.0% which is dependent upon the earlier outlined payout ratio. Hence, I think that Axos Financial is currently a buy when accounting for the current share price of $65.38.

Conclusion

Axos Financial has a solid history when considering the net interest margin, ranking as the first highest amongst similar banks over the past decade at 4.2%. This exemplifies the fact that the bank can achieve above-average spreads between what is received in internet income versus what is paid out to customers in interest expenses. Despite the bank delivering strong net interest margins, I am impressed that the bank has been able to keep nonperforming loans low, demonstrating skillful risk management while using moderate leverage. When it comes to the efficiency ratio, Axos Financial stands out by surpassing industry norms, demonstrating control over overhead costs. Data from the most recent quarter reflects an efficiency ratio of 46%, putting AX below the efficiency ratio benchmark of 60%. In addition, the management team has been able to drive strong ROA and ROE, showcasing their ability to increase the bank’s intrinsic value over time. Furthermore, in the previous 12 months, ROE has undergone a negative growth rate. When analyzing this bank, alongside my valuation, I forecast an expected yearly return of 18.0%, making AX a buy.

data.title: Why An Intel-TSMC Marriage Makes Sense
data.description:
On one of the worst days for the stock market in years and amidst the tariff chaos unleashed by the Trump administration, a fascinating report came down the wire that Intel (NASDAQ:INTC)(NEOE:INTC:CA) and Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM)(OTC:TSMWF) had agreed on a preliminary basis to create a joint venture that would operate some of Intel's foundries. This is a real curveball that has interesting implications for the future of the chip-making industry. Though many details are yet to be released, I think this arrangement has the potential to be beneficial to both parties.

During a crazy 24 hours that looked like it couldn't get a crazier, a quite reputable tech news publication called The Information reported that Intel and TSMC had agreed on an early framework for a joint venture that would operate some of Intel's fabs. Intel would likely provide much of the capital expenditures and would retain a significant stake, TSMC would provide training and expertise in exchange for 20%, and other as yet unnamed US semiconductor companies would have stakes as well, likely in exchange for additional capital and other commitments.

Needless to say, this is an unprecedented shift in the chip manufacturing industry. While on the surface, it appears like there are conflicting interests here that would leave such a deal dead in the water, there have been some developments in recent months and years that appear to have forced this outcome. More on this later.

While this is certainly a major event, it isn't exactly a complete surprise. The Trump administration has been pushing for such an outcome for months, and Reuters reported a few weeks ago that TSMC had even approached Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), and Qualcomm (QCOM) in an effort to pitch their investment in such a JV. Well, it seems that these negotiations have progressed fairly quickly!

Okay, so we've mentioned a lot of companies that all have competing interests here, so I think the best way to unravel potential winners and losers would be to evaluate each company's perspective. Let's start with the downtrodden dog everyone wants a piece of: Intel.

For some quick context, after long being an INTC bear during its foundry struggles amid its battles with AMD a few years ago, I switched to the bull side after the company announced its major restructuring. In my last article at the end of January, I argued the stock was a Buy on a turnaround for its potentially lucrative manufacturing business and the enduring prowess of its chip design segment. That piece can be read here.

I think the benefits for Intel here are apparent: the company can avoid bleeding billions from its foundry business, survive even if its 18A node ends up being less successful than hoped, receive chipmaking knowledge and training from the foremost experts in the field, and get some relief on capital investment now that CHIPS Act funding has likely been sidelined. Exactly how much know-how TSMC will be contributing and whether this includes information on leading-edge nodes is unclear, but I think it's more likely that the company will focus more on getting Intel's nodes to sustainable yields rather than sharing its most confidential and valuable trade secrets.

There are a couple of downsides for Intel too, in my opinion. The main downside is that this JV would essentially signal the end of an independent Intel foundry turnaround and the benefits that would come along with such an accomplishment. Though the odds weren't exactly stellar at this point, it does remove one of the high-ceiling bull scenarios. The second main downside, to me, is that letting TSMC peek under the hood will give it insight into Intel's processes that Intel will not receive into TSMC's. This likely has more short-term implications rather than long-term from a competitive advantage standpoint, but it is still a significant disclosure.

I think TSMC's position here is more nuanced. At first glance, it would seem to be a bad deal for the company -- it has to share knowledge and expertise with its main rival in order to manufacture chips that will likely steal business away from its own factories. In addition, TSMC has already committed $165 billion in US investment so why is it now concentrating even more investment in the country?

I think the answer is likely more geopolitical than anything. The Trump administration has been pushing TSMC to assist Intel for a while, gutted the CHIPS Act and instead got concessions from the company to invest an additional $100 billion in the US, and has threatened Taiwan with semiconductor tariffs. The US is also Taiwan's major military benefactor when it comes to keeping China from trying to seize the island by force. These factors all exert pressure for TSMC to assist Intel even when from a purely competitive or profit-driven perspective, this JV might not have been pursued.

With that said, I do see some benefits here. First, by owning 20% of this JV, TSMC is hedging its competitive risk -- even if Intel succeeds with 18A and creates a competitive node, its success will be partially owned by the company it would be competing against. Second, the JV allows TSMC to hedge political risk as well since this outcome would likely insulate it from the Trump administration's ire and would go a long way towards securing a tariff exemption. Third, as I mentioned earlier, in exchange for bailing out its spiraling rival, TSMC will get a valuable look into Intel's technologies and facilities that could prove useful in its own designs.

The last perspective here is that of the chip design companies who, in a vacuum, would likely not be too fond of the prospect of TSMC becoming intertwined with its primary rival. Not that Intel was lighting up the industry with design wins for its leading-edge nodes, but more competition is good for buyers whereas consolidation is good for the sellers. However, offering these companies a stake in the JV to operate these foundries mitigates some of those concerns; by investing in management of manufacturing facilities, these companies can likely secure capacity at lower costs with the caveat that there is much work to be done to get production up and running at scale.

Further, in exchange for their stakes in the JV, it's likely that these companies will have to commit to manufacturing at least some of their chips at JV-run plants for a certain number of years in order to get them off the ground and bootstrap the foundry business (à la AMD spinning off GlobalFoundries). While TSMC will own a stake, I still think this move would help to break some of the company's current pricing power leverage over the industry, and companies like Nvidia and Broadcom would love to see that happen.

So could this be the rare win-win-win for all involved? I think so, but perhaps not at equal magnitudes. I think the biggest winner here is Intel, followed by the chip design companies, and then TSMC ekes out a benefit, though mostly geopolitical.

Investor Takeaway

I'll preface this section with the warning that the current market environment is and will likely remain extremely volatile for the foreseeable future. Tariffs, counter-tariffs, and an impending economic slowdown are all weighing on the market and individual stocks will get caught in the crossfire even if there are bullish developments. The following recommendations are for long-term investors only. I don't claim to know what will happen in the short-term so invest at your own risk.

For long-term investors, I think this development adds to the bull thesis for INTC. I have not been a fan of a spin-off since I think Intel's foundry business is one of its main core advantages, so I think this JV idea is a good compromise and could end up being the beginning of the turnaround for which shareholders have been waiting. With TSMC assisting Intel fabs to run efficiently and scale up complicated nodes, the odds of stemming the cash bleed and returning to consistent profitability have increased significantly, in my opinion.

While this agreement is still preliminary, and I think it enhances the company's prospects, I think Intel is well-positioned regardless of the outcome. New CEO Lip-Bu Tan has espoused the foundry as the backbone of Intel, and I think this remains the case with or without the JV. I am re-iterating a Buy on INTC.

As for TSM, I continue to see the stock as a buy and hold forever candidate as its cutting-edge technology and operational excellence give it a competitive advantage even if it helps Intel get back on its feet in exchange for favorable treatment from the US government. While I don't think the company would have chosen to pitch this JV without pressure, I do still see benefits for TSMC and certainly don't think it negatively affects the long-term bull thesis. I am re-iterating a Strong Buy on TSM.

Thanks for reading and best of luck!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLV: My Method For Navigating These Pullbacks
data.description:
Net of short-term shocks, I believe there’s significant value in the healthcare segment.

That’s why, when the S&P 500 pulls back, I immediately start looking for potential bottom prices.

Using the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV) as a benchmark for the sector, I think the best way to navigate is through the multiples method, because it relies on publicly available data, widely used by reputable research firms and investment banks, making it both credible and, more importantly, effective in summarizing a sector’s overall direction.

Here are the reference targets I use for XLV.

Why XLV

I’ve already covered this ETF in previous analyses, I've used it several times as a benchmark for the U.S. healthcare sector because I find it highly representative.

For new readers, I’d like to provide this quick recap, just a list of key points to keep in mind when talking about XLV:

  • It’s an ETF with $38.42 billion in AUM, the most popular in its category, with an expense ratio of 0.09%, highly competitive, and a dividend yield of 1.48%.

    • It holds 63 U.S.-only stocks, which makes it, in my view, particularly well-suited for the type of analysis I’m doing here.
    • Peers generally have higher expense ratios and haven’t delivered better performance. I discussed this here.
    • That said, if you’re looking for diversification and performance, I always like to highlight the equal-weighted alternative, RSPH, which has shown a surprisingly strong Sharpe ratio. It’s one of the rare cases (alongside the industrial segment, which I covered here) where the equal-weight version has managed to compete with the market-weighted one.

My opinion

As of April, XLV’s forward P/E stands at 17x, down from 18x in December 2024, but not as sharply as in other sectors we’ve analyzed (see tech, for example).

At 18x, XLV was trading at a valuation higher than 79% of forward P/E observations over the past 10 years, and 69% over the past 30 years. At 17x, it’s closer to the average, above only 60% of the past 30 years’ forward P/Es.

Why do I start from this? Because while XLV’s price has dropped by 7% from its December 31 high, Q1 earnings for companies reported so far have grown by 35.9% (close to the expected 40%).(Note: these figures are not yet fully representative, as reporting is still ongoing.)

Meanwhile, EPS growth expectations for 2025 have declined by only 8.96% since December. That’s a relatively small adjustment, considering that the overall decline in 2025 EPS growth expectations for the S&P 500 was 19%.

So, point one: EPS expectations for the healthcare sector remain strong, at least stronger than those of the S&P 500.

If the S&P 500 is undergoing a mean reversion, which is the assumption behind much of my recent analysis, I ask myself: Does healthcare significantly shift the P/E distribution upward for the S&P 500? And the answer is: no.

The forward P/E of the healthcare sector (i.e., XLV) places in just the 17th percentile relative to the S&P 500, which means XLV’s forward P/E is higher than just the bottom 17% of P/E values in the S&P 500 distribution.

Thus, the actual danger of more severe contraction is elsewhere, directed across information technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials, all of which had as of December 31 forward P/Es in the 80th percentile vs the S&P 500’s 10yr and 30yr historical distributions.

Not surprisingly, if we look at performance: XLK has dropped by 17%, XLY by 20%, while XLV only by 9%.

So, point two: relative valuations (forward P/Es) in the healthcare sector are not extremely out of historical range.

My target price

Instinctively, I would maintain the current growth expectations for the healthcare sector in 2025 (18%), but given recent evidence showing an increase in negative earnings guidance, I prefer to take a more conservative approach.

How? By assuming a drop in earnings similar to the average decline seen in the S&P 500 (–19%) from the December 31 peak.

Applying that discount to the healthcare sector’s earnings growth rate gives us a projected earnings growth by the end of 2025 of 16%.

As for the P/E, since there’s so much talk around “mean reversion,” I’ll use a forward P/E average of 16x as a benchmark.

Currently, the forward P/E is 17x, with a 12-month EPS growth rate of 18%, but for the purpose of this valuation, I’m applying a target forward P/E of 16x and a revised EPS growth rate of 16%.

Using a simple proportion, this gives us a target price of $132, just over 5% below the current price of $140.

So I’m essentially assuming an implied PEG ratio that remains constant.

Risk

A 5% drop isn’t that far off. So, I’ll say it right away, I'm inclined to confirm a HOLD rating.

This rating is based on assumptions that are, let’s say, “hard to fully embrace”: an EPS growth rate in line with expectations (still, 16% is not a small number).

Naturally, if we assume a constant PEG ratio, a recession scenario would lead to lower EPS growth expectations, and thus lower price targets.

However, aside from the tariffs, at this time there are no credible EPS contraction estimates in the healthcare sector that I feel confident enough to use for setting concrete price targets.

Or rather, Q1 doesn’t show them. And since the impact of tariffs will become more evident over the next few months (with tariffs often being among the top suspects behind a recession), I believe Q2 will be key to understanding whether current EPS figures are actually realistic.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I currently see no reason to issue a downgrade on the rating.

In fact, healthcare may prove to be more defensive than other S&P 500 sectors, in my view.

The reasons are simple: EPS forecasts still show solid growth, and P/E ratios are not as stretched compared to other sectors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Liberation Hangover
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder

The Fed repricing is already providing room for longer end rates to drop lower

US rates markets continued to reprice in the wake of “Liberation Day”. Disappointing ISM services data adds to the gloom, with all eyes now turning to the jobs data ahead. The market is now pricing in 100bp of Federal Reserve rate cuts through January next year. Not starting immediately in May, but with a firm view that the next cut will arrive in June, giving the Fed time to read the data and adjust its bearings.

For the long end, the repricing of the Fed is relevant. It means that the trough in the SOFR strip, which we thought provided an implicit floor to long-end rates, is dropping. It has come down from the 3.5% area to now around 3.1%. At the long end, the 10Y US SOFR rate has come down to below 3.6% from a peak of 3.95% at the end of last week. As a result, the 10Y UST yield just about broke below the symbolic 4% handle.

The market's Fed view implies delayed impact expectations. It can mean that the rates market will continue trading with a bullish bias for now. A stronger jobs market will likely have less impact than a confirmation of weakening prospects that still have room to morph into a recession view. And for today, our economist does see the risk of a weaker jobs report than the consensus.

EUR markets are focused on the growth impact and less concerned about inflation than the ECB

When the European Central Bank last met on 6 March, it cut rates and kept an easing bias with an openness to pause. The changed wording on the restrictiveness of its policy rates was already a compromise amid diverging views in the Governing Council. Since then, the ECB’s policymaking is being made more difficult not only by the tariff announcement but also by Germany’s fiscal rethink last month. A surge higher in long-end rates back then had prompted doves to come out in favour of continuing to cut policy rates to offset the more immediate financial tightening effect.

Market rates are now lower again, but overall sentiment has clearly taken a hit on the US tariff announcement. Still, some officials have called for caution amid a more uncertain inflation outlook. The 20% tariff rate on the EU is slated to come into effect on 9 April (the universal 10% tariff is applied on 5 April), so in theory, it gives room for last-minute negotiations and deals, but the ECB should have some clarity ahead of the 17 April decision.

In the market’s thinking, an April ECB cut looks very likely still. The implied probability of just below 70% is not as high though as some days ago, before a Bloomberg report pointed to remaining divisions within the Council. But the market's focus is clearly on the weaking growth, and it is eyeing a growing possibility of a third cut this year to bring the depo rate to 1.75%.

Looking at the entire curve, it has been the 5y part that outperformed in the wake of the tariff announcements. 5y swaps were close to 9bp lower on the day, while surrounding 2y and 10y rates declined by a bit more than 7bp. 30y rates came down by 'only' 4bp. We think the steepening bias will remain the structural undertone, with the European and German spending pledges in the background. Temporary flattening episodes on the back of risk-off bouts are possible as the ECB is already priced aggressively.

Today's events and market view

The market will remain sensitive to any signs of trade negotiations and deals to stave off the impact of the impending tariffs. In data, all eyes are on the US jobs report where the consensus is looking for a payrolls' increase of 140k, down slightly from last month's 151k, and an unchanged unemployment rate of 4.1%. There is no clear skew in the individual forecasts, ranging from 80k to 200k. After the release, Fed Chair Jerome Powell takes the stage speaking on the economic outlook at a conference, giving him the opportunity to react both to Trump’s tariff announcements and the jobs data.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Rivian: Trump Tariffs Silver Lining (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) shares are down about 4.63% over the last month and are up a little less than 7% in the last six months. Despite also being down about 13.31% year-to-date, I am now more bullish on Rivian, and I’m upgrading shares to a strong buy for the first time in months.

After seeing their strong Q1 deliveries I think the EV maker has finally turned a corner after what I believe were multiple false starts. One of the biggest factors that went into my rating upgrade was not even within the company, however. I think the new round of tariffs announced today by this administration is actually going to be a net positive for the EV startup.

Yesterday, President Donald Trump added to his 25% tariff on imported vehicles by announcing a slew of tariffs on multiple nations intended to help net out the US trade deficit. While I am not sure if these tariffs will work, in the short run I expect them to actually help Rivian. One of Rivian’s biggest sticking points is cost. Many of its competitor’s EVs and ICE vehicles just got 25% more expensive.

I view this as a strong catalyst. Rivian is a U.S.-based manufacturer and assembler of its EVs, so I think that the company will have a much smaller fallout from this trade war than many of its competitors. For companies like Volkswagen who want US exposure without the tariffs on the way in (from importing many of their own vehicles) Rivian is a solid answer. I think the case for Rivian just got better.

While I believe Rivian still has a long way to go before they are profitable like their peer Tesla (TSLA) I think this regime of tariffs will help give them a real chance. Shares now appear undervalued.

With this, I now believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

While the market (and Rivian) are falling this week as a result of these aggressive tariffs, I think there is a silver lining for Rivian, with the EV maker conducting 100% of their assembly in the US. This is the biggest reason why I’m doing this follow up coverage. I want to point out how tariffs on their competitors' imported vehicles will impact Rivian.

Rivian posted stronger-than-expected Q4 gross margins, including a reported gross profit in Q4 2024 and reported strong deliveries for Q1 2025. I think the company’s cost controls and production efficiencies are starting to bear fruit after a challenging period. We may not be out of the woods yet, but I think they’re close.

On this same note, I believe Rivian is doing a fantastic job narrowing its strategic focus and shredding non-core projects. Their recent spin-off of Rivian’s micromobility division into Also Inc. (with a $105 million external investment) has helped streamline operations.

The company is getting lean and aggressive. Just in time for a trade war that will help justify their end prices better. Rivian will suffer from supply chain shocks (like everyone) but they should net out well.

Trump Tariffs Could Be A Silver Lining

While President Trump this week unveiled a series of tariffs as a part of his “Liberation day” plan to place tariffs on dozens of countries in an attempt to help the US balance out its trade deficits, I actually want to focus on his tariff announcement from last month.

According to the official executive order signed by Trump on March 26:

President Donald J. Trump signed a proclamation invoking Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 to impose a 25% tariff on imports of automobiles and certain automobile parts, addressing a critical threat to U.S. national security.

The 25% tariff will be applied to imported passenger vehicles (sedans, SUVs, crossovers, minivans, cargo vans) and light trucks, as well as key automobile parts (engines, transmissions, powertrain parts, and electrical components), with processes to expand tariffs on additional parts if necessary.

While on the surface automotive tariffs may seem like a headwind, Rivian’s business is anti-fragile to this tariff chaos thanks to their 100% assembled in the USA line up of vehicles. This is well above the industry average.

According to GlobalData, 46.6% of all vehicles sold in the United States last year were imported. As the chart below shows, while a majority of US lightweight vehicles are made here in the US, roughly ½ still trace their origin back to international origins. Most of these imports come from nearby countries like Mexico (16.2%) and Canada (7.2%), meaning they are not subject to the reciprocal tariffs that the Trump administration put on many partner countries this week.

However, regardless of reciprocal tariffs, major exporting countries like Mexico (16.2%), Korea (8.6%), Japan (8.2%) and Canada (7.2%) now face a 25% cost increase on their imported vehicles. This places those countries and the vehicles they import at a major pricing disadvantage. For countries like China, the entire country’s export base to the US is now subject to 34% tariffs on top of 25% tariffs for automobiles.

With tariffs driving up competitors’ costs, I see a “pricing umbrella” under which Rivian can boost its average selling price (especially when competing against Chinese assembled EVs). As competitors are forced to raise their prices to combat this minimum 25% tariff, this will allow Rivian to also maintain ((Or)) increase its prices at a marginal rate to take advantage of this opportunity.

Add to this their stronger than expected Q1 deliveries (14,611 vehicles vs 12,380 expected) and you get a strong setup. Rivian was beating expectations before tariffs arrived to help make their vehicles more competitive. This is key.

Valuation

With the company now facing a net favorable trade environment and better than expected deliveries, I think shares have room to run from here. If we look at the EV makers' forward price-to-book ratio, we see that Rivian has a forward price to book of 2.90. When compared to the sector median of 2.28, this is only a 27.42% premium. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Rivian a grade of a C on this metric.

With a strong cash position and tangible assets, I think that this modest premium is more than justified. I also believe that the monetization of non-core assets like the Also Inc. spin-off further adds to their balance sheet. Really, I think they’re in a solid spot.

On the growth front, Rivian’s forward revenue projections look strong. The company has a projected forward revenue growth rate of 19.25%, which is 498.69% higher than the sector median of 3.22%. Seeking Alpha Quant gives the company a grade of an A on this metric.

In my opinion, this robust revenue growth forecast warrants a higher forward price to sales multiple. Rivian does currently trade at higher sales multiples, with a forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.61 compared to a sector median of 0.84. However, I think this 212.09% premium is more than justified given that, by buying shares at this premium, you get access to a company that has top-line growth that is 498.69% higher than the sector median.

With this, I think shares should trade at a 300% premium to the sector median. If we saw shares converge on this forward Price/sales ratio this would represent 41.44% upside from here.

Risks

While I think Rivian has a unique opportunity to net gain from this tariff plan from the Trump administration, they’re far from out of the woods. Last time I wrote on Rivian in mid-February, I rated shares as a hold. Some of my concerns have receded (more expensive competitor vehicles, and higher delivery volumes), but there is still a core concern: will Rivian sell enough vehicles to get profitable?

Tariffs are good for Rivian if Rivian can execute and get their supply chain under control (control costs). Fortunately, the company has put an intense focus on keeping their supply chain lean. I talked about this in research I wrote in late 2023.

With this, Rivian is fighting off their competitive pricing risks, and their supply chain risks. Really, I think the biggest unmitigated risk from here is their DOE (Department of Energy) funding.

Rivian has a conditional $6.6 billion DOE loan that could face setbacks as part of the new administration’s Department of Government Efficiency. This loan is supposed to be used to help the financing of building new factories here in the US. I think this is the silver lining.

While this administration is clearly anti-EV’s on the whole, I think their desire to build more factories in the US trumps this (no pun intended). I now believe the company will be able to keep their loan from the DOE. In all, Rivian should have up to $10.1 billion of liquidity on hand (per their Q4 call). I think this will go a long way to make sure they can ride out the effects of Tariffs and come ahead.

Bottom Line

While I think the new tariffs implemented by President Trump are going to be a drag for some of Rivian’s components, I think overall this could be a net game changer for Rivian. A month and a half ago, when I last wrote on the company, I was a hold on shares. But I think this is a huge development for the EV maker, which is why I’m upgrading my rating on the company to a strong buy.

I think this new tariff regime has tilted the competitive playing field in Rivian’s favor as foreign EVs and foreign ICE vehicles deal with a 25% markup in their prices, making Rivian’s MSRP comparatively more attractive. As I mentioned earlier, nearly half of U.S. auto sales come from imports, and most competitors will now be faced with this 25% bump in price. Meanwhile, Rivian, which builds its vehicles in the United States, does not have to deal with this tariff issue directly. Yes components will be more expensive, but their net increase in price should be lower than their competitors’ net increase in price.

Rivian’s domestic manufacturing base, now net favorable tariff environment, and leaner operational focus gives them a rare advantage. This allows Rivian to concentrate on scaling production (R1 ramp and R2 launch) without facing the same pricing pressures as its competitors. This also bodes well for their investor (Volkswagen) who now has exposure to an automaker that is heavily concentrated on US manufacturing.

While the market (and economy) may be turbulent from here, I believe Rivian’s outlook has improved over the last month or so. Both internal operations and external tariff policy could work in Rivian’s favor.

With this, I believe shares are now a strong buy (up from a hold).

data.title: Liberation Day: Market Uncertainty, Global Growth Concerns, And U.S. Recession Fears
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

What is the announcement, and how does it compare to market expectations?

President Trump announced that he would apply a minimum 10% tariff on all exporters to the U.S., and multiple countries will face an additional tariff that equals half the ratio of the U.S. bilateral trade deficit with the country divided by U.S. imports from that country. The list of countries is extensive, with some countries facing tariffs of over 40%.

Of the major global economies, China will face a 54% tariff (a 34% “reciprocal” tariff on top of the already announced 20%), Europe will face a tariff of 20%, and Japan 24%. Several countries, including the UK and Australia, will have the minimum 10% applied to them.

Canada and Mexico were excluded from the list, suggesting they will continue to face 25% tariffs for non-USMCA-compliant goods, but no additional levies. Exemptions have been given to energy products, sectors that are already facing additional tariffs (such as aluminum and steel), and other sectors that are subject to investigations - reducing the overall impact.

However, many of these exemptions are likely only temporary, as additional sectoral tariffs are likely to be applied. Regarding timing, 10% tariffs come into effect this weekend and the higher tariffs will be introduced next week.

Previously announced measures had increased the U.S. average tariff rate to 11%, the highest since the 1940s. Yesterday’s announcement raised the tariff rate even further to around 24%, the highest since 1908, and was meaningfully more aggressive than the broad market had expected.

Unpacking the immediate market reaction

With the initial announcement proving to be more aggressive than expected, the market responded overwhelmingly negatively, with stock futures trading lower and the U.S. dollar weakening further. U.S. Treasurys have rallied only slightly as the market tries to digest the conflicting growth and inflation impacts.

For the time being, market expectations for Fed rates have not shifted, with three cuts still expected this year. However, some forecasters have already taken out one Fed cut from their 2025 forecasts, so market pricing may begin to shift. Fed Chair Powell speaks tomorrow and may offer some insights as to how the central bank is viewing this very challenging policy predicament.

Next steps

While “Liberation Day” helped reduce some tariff uncertainty, it hasn’t resolved it entirely. Markets are still waiting to see whether additional sector-specific tariffs will be announced and whether countries will choose to negotiate or retaliate.

Negotiation could lower some of the higher individual tariff rates, while retaliation risks triggering an escalatory cycle. Early reports from Europe and China are suggestive of retaliation, though both are likely to respond cautiously as they weigh the least damaging path forward.

For some countries, negotiations will be particularly difficult. In the EU’s case, for example, value-added tax (VAT) is viewed as a fair, non-discriminatory tax since it applies equally to domestic and imported goods.

Ultimately, whether through negotiation or retaliation, the U.S. announcement points to a renewed stretch of uncertainty over the final scope, level and timing of tariffs.

How does this impact the macro outlook for the U.S.?

The size and scope of the tariff announcement pale in comparison to punitive trade actions seen during the 2018 Trade War and underscore the heightened economic disruption that is likely to emerge. The overall impact is likely to be severe to the consumer, with the tariff increase representing the largest tax hike seen in the U.S. in modern history.

While the administration’s stated goal of the tariffs - reshoring manufacturing and the resulting capex boost to the economy - is certainly possible, the reality is that the process will likely take years if it happens at all. In the meantime, the steep tariffs on imports are likely to be an immediate drag on the economy, with limited short-term benefit.

One item that market participants will be waiting to learn more about is pro-growth economic tailwinds. An optimistic scenario would be for new fiscal measures to be announced, potentially funded by the $600-700bn in annual tariff revenue in the near-term.

Uses for the tariff revenues could include helping to reduce the deficit, thereby driving down bond yields, or financing tax cuts. For example, corporate tax cuts would stimulate on-shoring, while income tax cuts could provide important support for lower-income households, limiting the tariff impact on the U.S. consumer. To date, nothing has been formally proposed, but it would provide an important support to both the U.S. economy and markets if this begins to take shape.

Ahead of the announcement, most analysts had revised their U.S. growth forecasts lower but still did not expect a recession. With the size and scope of what was announced, however, there are now likely to be further downward revisions as economists recalculate the increase in the average tariff rate.

Our initial estimates suggest that U.S. GDP growth is likely to see an initial hit of around 2.5%, with the fallout potentially larger if some trade partners retaliate. (This is an increase from our prior estimate of about a 1.5% hit to growth, following the trade actions announced before April 2.)

We will review our GDP forecast (which had been initially lowered in early March to show GDP growth falling meaningfully below trend but skirting recession) but early calculations suggest that a U.S. recession could materialize unless:

  1. At least some tariff rates are reduced in the coming months; and,
  2. The Fed resumes policy rate cuts; and,
  3. Growth-friendly measures are introduced later this year, such as tax cuts and deregulation.

Impact on inflation

The most meaningful impact of the tariffs is likely to fall on U.S. consumers. With the most significant tariff increases targeting countries central to the U.S. supply chain for consumer goods - China, Vietnam, Taiwan, and Cambodia - households should expect higher prices across a wide range of everyday items.

The introduction of the 10% blanket tariffs on other trading partners further limits the potential for substitution, leaving importers - and ultimately consumers - with little choice but to absorb the full cost of rising prices in the near term.

As a result, inflation pressures are set to intensify. Current forecasts likely underestimate the upside risk, with estimates suggesting that tariffs alone could add as much as 1.4% to inflation.

With roughly 6% of personal goods consumption tied to imports, core CPI could easily trend back toward 4% by year-end, reversing much of the recent inflation progress and weighing on real purchasing power.

The combination of slowing growth and rising inflation creates an increasingly uneasy macro environment - one that bears some resemblance to stagflationary conditions, even if it doesn't fully meet the definition. This mix adds complexity to the policy outlook.

How might the Fed’s policy outlook be impacted?

From the Fed’s perspective, the recent inflation pickup makes their policy decision-making even more uncomfortable. With price stability still not fully achieved, and tariffs threatening to push prices higher, policymakers may not be able to provide as much monetary support as the growth picture requires, and could even bind them from cutting rates at all.

If the Fed believes that softer growth will exert downward pressure on inflation in the medium term (provided long-term inflation expectations remain anchored), the path to policy easing is still plausible. We continue to expect two to three rate cuts this year, but the path to easing has become narrower and more uncertain.

What do the tariffs mean for Europe and China?

Europe

We initially lowered our 2025 Euro area GDP growth forecast from 0.9% to 0.7%, assuming a 10% targeted tariff on European exports. A 20% blanket tariff now points to a 0.9 percentage point direct drag on growth - with further downside risk if Europe retaliates. As a result, another downgrade to the European growth outlook is likely.

The inflation impact remains uncertain, but the downside risks to growth suggest the ECB’s policy path is relatively straightforward. While recent weeks have seen some hesitation around the need for additional rate cuts, the combination of weaker growth prospects and a stronger euro makes a rate cut at the ECB’s April meeting highly likely. If a recession becomes more probable, multiple additional cuts could follow.

Note that Germany’s historic fiscal policy shift should at least provide a positive offset over the medium term, helping Europe to contend with the tariff headwinds. In addition, it is possible that Europe introduces new targeted measures to support deeply impacted sectors.

China

China’s 54% tariff is close to the original 60% tariff that President Trump had initially threatened. The 54% is also still larger than most forecasters were expecting. With tariff rates on several other Asian economies rising to levels that will likely tip them into recession (for example, Vietnam has been hit with a nearly 50% tariff), it will be challenging for China to re-route their exports.

Additional stimulus from policymakers, both monetary and fiscal, is likely to be announced to offset the tariff impact. At the recent March NPC meeting, the announced fiscal package was modestly expansive, but policymakers noted that they were committed to increasing the policy response if necessary. The next watchpoint will be later this month, when the politburo holds its quarterly meeting on the economy.

The early read is that export headwinds will intensify, weighing more heavily on growth. However, additional stimulus measures are likely to cushion the blow by lifting domestic consumption. Even so, we expect to revise down our 2025 GDP growth forecast for China - shifting from a strong 4.5% to a more modest 4.2-4.3%, depending on the size and speed of policy support.

Risk asset outlook

Unfortunately, Wednesday’s announcement left tariff uncertainty firmly in place and was not the clearing event many had hoped for. The stagflationary nature of the shock implies that markets are likely to remain risk-off for the time being.

With over 41% of S&P 500 revenues generated outside the U.S., markets will be focused on how countries respond to the announcement and if they begin to consider ways to strengthen their economies against these headwinds.

Notably, Wednesday’s announcement potentially paved the way for the Trump administration to turn their attention to some growth-friendly measures, such as tax policy and deregulation.

A constructive announcement on these policies could steady market sentiment and enable investors to look beyond the destructive trade headlines that have dominated markets for the past two months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Stocks Plunge As The Market Understood What Tariffs Are: A Tax On Corporate Profit Margins
data.description:
Trump explained it clearly yesterday, and markets understood. But companies have known it all along.

Stocks tanked today as markets understood what tariffs actually are: A tax on corporate profit margins that companies will have a very hard time passing on, which is why companies hate tariffs so much. If they could pass them on easily, they wouldn’t care about tariffs; their revenues would go up by the amount of the tariffs, and their profit margins in percentage terms could be maintained and their stocks would keep rising. But that’s not the case.

Companies have known this all along. Markets figured it out today.

The S&P 500 fell 4.84% today. Markets have been so spoiled. Despite the decline of 12% from the high, the S&P 500 is still up 4.8% year-over-year, and by 31% from two years ago.

The Nasdaq fell 5.97% today and is down 18% from the high. But it’s still up 3.1% year-over-year, and by 37% from two years ago.

Stock price gains have been so huge for so long that these declines are practically minor. Stocks have been at such precariously high levels that any little thing would knock them off their perch.

Longer-term Treasury securities rallied and yields fell, with the 10-year yield dropping to 4.03%, a classic safe-haven trade.

Trump explained tariffs yesterday, and markets understood

President Trump outlined the new strategy of imposing layered tariffs on corporate profit margins yesterday in the Rose Garden, and encouraged companies to dodge those tariffs by shifting production to the US.

One of the countries he singled out was Ireland, where US Big Pharma companies have set up entities that make profits that are taxable under the low corporate income tax rates in Ireland but are not taxed by the US. And then they sell those products in the US without profit, thereby dodging corporate income taxes in the US. Not paying income taxes in the US has widened the already big-fat profit margins of Big Pharma.

Other companies are doing the same thing. And so, the US had this huge $87 billion trade deficit in goods with tiny Ireland last year, and Trump outlined how tariffs would change the math for this strategy.

The #1 goal of tariffs is to bring production to the US and end the system of “globalization at the expense of the US economy.”

The entire auto industry has already been expanding and building factories in the US in recent years. Recently, numerous automakers have announced plans to build more factories in the US and produce more vehicles and components in the US. Same in the semiconductor industry and other industries.

It takes years to plan a factory, make a deal with local governments, buy the property, build the plant, equip the plant, and ramp up production, so this is not a short-term thing. All new factories in the US are highly automated, and employees are highly qualified, many of them are engineers.

The prior Trump tariffs were finalized in late 2018, after numerous changes, exemptions, carveouts, etc. By 2019, companies strategized and decided on how to deal with them long term, including by planning to build factories in the US. Then came Covid in early 2020 and this process was put on hold. But in mid-2021, after the economy reopened, the first projects put all their ducks in a row, and the spending on the construction of factories started to soar, and it was the beginning of a historic construction boom.

This was further powered by the Biden Administration’s incentives. The CHIPS Act was passed in August 2022, and the first funds started flowing at the end of 2024.

And now come the new incentives to build factories in the US - in the form of the new tariffs.

This is a hugely important development for the long-term strength of the US economy. Starting up a factory is a massive investment with immediate and long-term benefits for the US economy, including secondary and tertiary benefits.

The chart below shows this boom of the amounts spent on construction of manufacturing facilities. But they do not include the investments in equipment, such as industrial robots, computers, etc. that dwarf the construction costs of the building.

The #2 goal of tariffs is to impose taxes on corporate profits if companies decide to import, which forces companies to pay taxes that they might otherwise have dodged, such as Big Pharma and Big Tech that run their profits through Ireland and other financial centers and pay zero income taxes in the US on those profits sheltered overseas. Trump explained that yesterday, which may be why Apple (AAPL) tanked 9% today.

Tariffs are bad for stocks because they’re tax on corporate profit margins

We know that, based on how stocks got hit in 2018 through the tariffs finally imposed then, and the S&P 500 went down 20%. I said in the headline on February 3: “What Trump’s Tariffs Did Last Time (2018-2019): No Impact on Inflation, Doubled Receipts from Customs Duties, and Hit Stocks”

Tariffs are bad for stocks because they extract taxes from companies, many of which would otherwise not pay income taxes in the US. Hence, lower stock prices. The market understands that now just fine, despite the relentless BS in the media that tariffs are sales tax for consumers.

Passing on higher costs to consumers is very hard. It tends to cause sales to plunge, as Americans hate higher prices. And then, with sales dropping, the price increases are rolled back.

The Trump administration is refusing to let the addiction to inflated stock prices dictate essential long-term US economic policies. And markets are figuring it out.

Services exports don’t compensate for imports of manufactured goods

Services exports of the US fall mostly into two categories:

  1. Foreign tourism in the US - the money foreigners spend on lodging, restaurants, rental cars, airfares, etc., which are fairly low-level activities, unlike manufacturing of high-value goods, such as cars or semiconductors.
  2. Sales overseas of IP, such as software, movies, etc., that has already been made for and sold in the US, and whose sales in other countries add profits for the selling companies but create little or no additional economic activity in the US. Which is why services exports don’t have a lot of effect on the US economy, unlike manufacturing.

In addition, the services surplus the US has is small compared to the gigantic deficit in goods.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
